title: Diffusion Models Beat GANs on Image Synthesis

paragraphs.text:
## **Diffusion Models Beat GANs on Image Synthesis**

**Prafulla Dhariwal** _[⇤]_ **Alex Nichol** _[⇤]_ OpenAI OpenAI `prafulla@openai.com alex@openai.com`

## **Abstract**

We show that diffusion models can achieve image sample quality superior to the current state-of-the-art generative models. We achieve this on unconditional image synthesis by finding a better architecture through a series of ablations. For conditional image synthesis, we further improve sample quality with classifier guidance: a simple, compute-efficient method for trading off diversity for fidelity using gradients from a classifier. We achieve an FID of 2.97 on ImageNet 128 _⇥_ 128, 4.59 on ImageNet 256 _⇥_ 256, and 7.72 on ImageNet 512 _⇥_ 512, and we match BigGAN-deep even with as few as 25 forward passes per sample, all while maintaining better coverage of the distribution. Finally, we find that classifier guidance combines well with upsampling diffusion models, further improving FID to 3.94 on ImageNet 256 _⇥_ 256 and 3.85 on ImageNet 512 _⇥_ 512.

## **1 Introduction**

Figure 1: Selected samples from our best ImageNet 512 _⇥_ 512 model (FID 3.85)

Over the past few years, generative models have gained the ability to generate human-like natural language [9], high-quality synthetic images [8, 34, 57] and highly diverse human speech and music [70, 17]. These models can be used in a variety of ways, such as generating images from text prompts [78, 56] or learning useful feature representations [18, 10]. While these models are already capable

> _⇤_ Equal contribution

35th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2021).

of producing realistic images and sound, there is still much room for improvement beyond the current state-of-the-art, and better generative models could have wide-ranging impacts on graphic design, games, music production, and countless other fields.

GANs [25] currently hold the state-of-the-art on most image generation tasks [8, 74, 34] as measured by sample quality metrics such as FID [29], Inception Score [61] and Precision [38]. However, some of these metrics do not fully capture diversity, and it has been shown that GANs capture less diversity than state-of-the-art likelihood-based models [57, 49, 48]. Furthermore, GANs are often difficult to train, collapsing without carefully selected hyperparameters and regularizers [8, 47, 7]. While GANs hold the state-of-the-art, their drawbacks make them difficult to scale and apply to new domains. As a result, much work has been done to achieve GAN-like sample quality with likelihood-based models [22, 57, 31, 48, 12]. While these models capture more diversity and are typically easier to scale and train than GANs, they still fall short in terms of visual fidelity. Furthermore, except for VAEs, sampling from these models is slower than GANs in terms of wall-clock time.

Diffusion models are a class of likelihood-based models which have recently been shown to produce high-quality images [63, 66, 31, 49] while offering desirable properties such as distribution coverage, a stationary training objective, and easy scalability. These models generate samples by gradually removing noise from a signal, and their training objective can be expressed as a reweighted variational lower-bound [31]. This class of models already holds the state-of-the-art [67] on CIFAR-10 [37], but still lags behind GANs on difficult generation datasets like LSUN and ImageNet. We hypothesize that this gap exists for at least two reasons: first, that the model architectures used by recent GAN literature have been heavily explored and refined; second, that GANs are able to trade off diversity for fidelity, producing high quality samples but not covering the whole distribution. We aim to bring these benefits to diffusion models, first by improving model architecture and then by devising a scheme for trading off diversity for fidelity.

The rest of the paper is organized as follows. In Section 2, we give a brief background of diffusion models based on Ho et al. [31] and the improvements from Nichol and Dhariwal [49] and Song et al. [64], and we describe our evaluation setup. In Section 3, we introduce simple architecture improvements that give a substantial boost to FID. In Section 4, we describe a method for using gradients from a classifier to guide a diffusion model during sampling. Finally, in Section 5 we show that models with our improved architecture achieve state-of-the-art on unconditional image synthesis tasks, and with classifier guidance achieve state-of-the-art on conditional image synthesis.

## **2 Background**

In this section, we provide a brief overview of diffusion models. For a more detailed mathematical description, we refer the reader to Appendix C. On a high level, diffusion models sample from a distribution by reversing a gradual noising process. In particular, sampling starts with noise _xT_ and produces gradually less-noisy samples _xT −_ 1 _, xT −_ 2 _, ..._ until reaching a final sample _x_ 0. In particular, a diffusion model learns to produce a slightly more “denoised” _xt−_ 1 from _xt_ . Ho et al. [31] parameterize this model using a function _✏✓_ ( _xt, t_ ) which predicts the noise component of a noisy sample _xt_ . To train this function, each sample in a minibatch is produced by randomly drawing a data sample _x_ 0, a timestep _t_ , and noise _✏_ , which together give rise to a noised sample _xt_ (Equation 3, Appendix C). The training objective is then _||✏✓_ ( _xt, t_ ) _− ✏||_[2] , i.e. a simple mean-squared error loss between the true noise and the predicted noise (Equation 12, Appendix C).

Ho et al. [31] show that, under reasonable assumptions, we can then model the denoising distribution _p✓_ ( _xt−_ 1 _|xt_ ) of _xt−_ 1 given _xt_ as a diagonal Gaussian _N_ ( _xt−_ 1; _µ✓_ ( _xt, t_ ) _,_ ⌃ _✓_ ( _xt, t_ )), where the mean _µ✓_ ( _xt, t_ ) can be calculated as a function of _✏✓_ ( _xt, t_ ) (Equation 13, Appendix C). Ho et al. [31] observe that the simple mean-squared error objective, _L_ simple, works better in practice than the actual variational lower bound _L_ vlb that can be derived from interpreting the denoising diffusion model as a VAE. They also note that training with this objective and using their corresponding sampling procedure is equivalent to the denoising score matching model from Song and Ermon [65], who use Langevin dynamics to sample from a denoising model trained with multiple noise levels to produce high quality image samples. We often use “diffusion models” as shorthand to refer to both classes of models.
Following the breakthrough work of Song and Ermon [65] and Ho et al. [31], several recent papers have proposed improvements to diffusion models. Nichol and Dhariwal [49] find that fixing

2

the variance ⌃ _✓_ ( _xt, t_ ) to a constant as done in Ho et al. [31] is sub-optimal for sampling with fewer diffusion steps, and propose to parameterize ⌃ _✓_ ( _xt, t_ ) as a neural network whose output _v_ is interpolated as ⌃ _✓_ ( _xt, t_ ) = exp( _v_ log _βt_ + (1 _− v_ ) log _β_[˜] _t_ ). Here, _βt_ and _β_[˜] _t_ (Equation 5, Appendix C) are the variances in Ho et al. [31] corresponding to upper and lower bounds for the reverse process variances. Additionally, Nichol and Dhariwal [49] propose a hybrid objective for training both _✏✓_ ( _xt, t_ ) and ⌃ _✓_ ( _xt, t_ ) using the weighted sum _L_ simple + _λL_ vlb. Learning the reverse process variances with their hybrid objective allows sampling with fewer steps without much drop in sample quality. We adopt this objective and parameterization, and use it throughout our experiments.

Song et al. [64] propose DDIM, which formulates an alternative non-Markovian noising process that has the same forward marginals as DDPM, but allows producing different reverse samplers by changing the variance of the reverse noise. By setting this noise to 0, they provide a way to turn any model _✏✓_ ( _xt, t_ ) into a deterministic mapping from latents to images, and find that this provides an alternative way to sample with fewer steps. We adopt this sampling approach when using fewer than 50 sampling steps, since Nichol and Dhariwal [49] found it to be beneficial in this regime.

**Sample Quality Metrics** : For comparing sample quality across models, we perform quantitative evaluations using the following metrics. While these metrics are often used in practice and correspond well with human judgement, they are not a perfect proxy, and finding better metrics for sample quality evaluation is still an open problem.

We use FID [29] as our default metric for overall sample quality comparisons as it captures both fidelity and diversity and has been the de facto standard metric for state-of-the-art generative models [33, 34, 8, 31]. We use Precision and Recall [38] as proxies for separately measuring fidelity and diversity, respectively. We include sFID [48] as a metric that better captures spatial relationships than FID, and also include Inception Score (IS) [61] as another proxy for fidelity. When comparing against other methods, we re-compute these metrics using public samples or models whenever possible. This is for two reasons: first, some papers [33, 34, 31] compare against arbitrary subsets of the training set which are not readily available; and second, subtle implementation differences can affect the resulting FID values [51]. For consistent comparisons, we use the full training set as the reference batch [29, 8], and evaluate metrics for all models using the same codebase.

## **3 Architecture Improvements**

Ho et al. [31] adopted the UNet architecture [58] for diffusion models, which Jolicoeur-Martineau et al. [32] found to substantially improve sample quality over the previous architectures [65, 39] used for denoising score matching. The UNet model uses a stack of residual layers and downsampling convolutions, followed by a stack of residual layers with upsampling convolutions, with skip connections connecting the layers with the same spatial size. In addition, they use a global attention layer at the 16 _⇥_ 16 resolution with a single head, and add a projection of the timestep embedding into each residual block. Song et al. [67] found that further changes to the UNet architecture improved performance on the CIFAR-10 [37] and CelebA-64 [40] datasets. We show the same result on ImageNet 128 _⇥_ 128, finding that architecture can indeed give a substantial boost to sample quality on a much larger and more diverse datasets at a higher resolution.

We explore the following architectural changes: increasing depth versus width, holding model size relatively constant; increasing the number of attention heads; using attention at 32 _⇥_ 32, 16 _⇥_ 16, and 8 _⇥_ 8 resolutions rather than only at 16 _⇥_ 16; using the BigGAN [8] residual block for upsampling and downsampling the activations, following [67]; and finally; rescaling residual connections with ~~_p_~~ 12[,] following [67, 33, 34].

We train models with the above architecture changes on ImageNet 128 _⇥_ 128 and compare them on FID, evaluated at two different points of training, in Table 1. Aside from rescaling residual connections, all of the other modifications improve performance and have a positive compounding effect. On wall-clock (Figure 5, Appendix A.1) we find that increased depth hurts training time most, so we opt not to use this change in further experiments. We also study other attention configurations that better match the Transformer architecture [72]. We try two configurations: constant attention heads, or constant channels per head. Table 2 shows our results, indicating that more heads or fewer channels per head improves FID. On wall-clock (Figure 5, Appendix A.1), we see that 64 channels is best so we opt to use 64 channels per head as our default. We note that this choice also better matches modern transformer architectures, and is on par with our other configurations in terms of final FID.
Table 2: Ablation of attention heads. More heads or lower channels per heads both improve FID. The base model was a smaller version of the best model from Table 1.

We also experiment with a layer [49] that we refer to as adaptive group normalization (AdaGN), which incorporates the timestep and class embedding into each residual block after a group normalization operation [75], similar to adaptive instance norm [33] and FiLM [54]. We define this layer as AdaGN( _h, y_ ) = _ys_ GroupNorm( _h_ )+ _yb_ , where _h_ is the intermediate activations of the residual block following the first convolution, and _y_ = [ _ys, yb_ ] is obtained from a linear projection of the timestep and class embedding. We had already seen AdaGN improve our earliest diffusion models, and so had included it by default in all our runs. We explicitly ablate this choice (Table 6, Appendix A.1), and find that FID becomes worse by 2.02 when we remove the adaptive group normalization layer.

variable width with 2 residual blocks per resolution, multiple heads with 64 channels per head, attention at 32, 16 and 8 resolutions, BigGAN residual blocks for up and downsampling, and adaptive group normalization for injecting timestep and class embeddings into residual blocks.

## **4**

In addition to employing well designed architectures, GANs for conditional image synthesis [45, 8] make heavy use of class labels. This often takes the form of class-conditional normalization statistics [20, 14] as well as discriminators with heads explicitly designed to behave like classifiers _p_ ( _y|x_ ) [46]. As further evidence that class information is crucial to the success of these models, Lucic et al. [42] find that it is helpful to generate synthetic labels when working in a label-limited regime. Given this observation for GANs, it makes sense to explore different ways to condition diffusion models on class labels. We already incorporate class information into adaptive group normalization layers (Section 3). Here, we explore a different approach: exploiting a classifier _p_ ( _y|x_ ) to improve a diffusion generator. Sohl-Dickstein et al. [63] and Song et al. [67] show one way to achieve this, wherein a pre-trained diffusion model can be conditioned using the gradients of a classifier. In particular, we can train a classifier _pφ_ ( _y|xt, t_ ) on noisy images _xt_ , and then use gradients _rxt_ log _pφ_ ( _y|xt, t_ ) to guide the diffusion sampling process towards an arbitrary class label _y_ .

For class conditional diffusion sampling, we reproduce the derivation from Sohl-Dickstein et al. [63] in Appendix D.2. For DDIM, we perform a score-based derivation in Appendix D.3 inspired by Song et al. [67]. The resulting sampling algorithms we use for guidance are Algorithms 1 and 2 respectively. Both algorithms incorporate class information by adding the gradients of a classifier to each sampling step with an appropriate step size. In these algorithms, we choose the notation

To apply guidance to a large scale generative task, we train models on ImageNet. Our classifier architecture is simply the downsampling trunk of the UNet model with an attention pool [55] at the 8x8 layer to produce the final output. We train these classifiers on the same noising distribution as the corresponding diffusion model, and also add random crops to reduce overfitting.

In initial experiments with unconditional ImageNet models, we found it necessary to scale the classifier gradients by a constant factor larger than 1. When using a scale of 1, we observed that the classifier assigned reasonable probabilities (around 50%) to the desired classes for the final samples, but these samples did not match the intended classes upon visual inspection. Scaling up the classifier gradients remedied this problem, and the class probabilities from the classifier increased to nearly 100%. Figure 2 shows an example of this effect. We see that scaling the gradients beyond 1.0 smoothly trades off recall (a measure of diversity) for higher precision and IS (measures of fidelity). Since FID and sFID depend on both diversity and fidelity, their best values are obtained at an intermediate point. We also compare our guidance with the truncation trick from BigGAN (Figure 6, Appendix A.2). We find that classifier guidance is strictly better than BigGAN-deep when trading off FID for Inception Score. Less clear cut is the precision/recall trade-off, which shows that classifier guidance is only a better choice up until a certain precision threshold, after which point it cannot achieve better precision.

## **5 Results**

To evaluate our improved model architecture on unconditional image generation, we train separate diffusion models on three LSUN [77] classes: bedroom, horse, and cat. To evaluate classifier guidance, we train conditional diffusion models on the ImageNet [59] dataset at 128 _⇥_ 128, 256 _⇥_ 256, and 512 _⇥_ 512 resolution.

Table 4 summarizes our results. ADM refers to our **a** blated **d** iffusion **m** odel, and ADM-G additionally uses classifier **g** uidance. Our diffusion models can obtain the best FID on each task, and the best sFID on all but one task. With the improved architecture, we already obtain state-of-the-art image generation on LSUN and ImageNet 64 _⇥_ 64. For higher resolution ImageNet, we observe that classifier guidance allows our models to substantially outperform the best GANs. These models obtain perceptual quality similar to GANs, while maintaining a higher coverage of the distribution as measured by recall, and can even do so using only 25 sampling steps. We also evaluate the computational requirements for training our models (Table 10, Appendix B), and find that we can obtain competitive sample quality while using the same or less compute than the corresponding BigGAN-deep or StyleGAN2 model.

We also compare guidance to using a two-stage upsampling stack. Nichol and Dhariwal [49] and Saharia et al. [60] train two-stage diffusion models by combining a low-resolution diffusion model with a corresponding upsampling diffusion model. In this approach, the upsampling model is trained to upsample images from the training set, and conditions on low-resolution images that are concatenated channel-wise to the model input using a simple interpolation (e.g. bilinear). During sampling, the low-resolution model produces a sample, and then the upsampling model is conditioned on this sample. This greatly improves FID on ImageNet 256 _⇥_ 256, but does not reach the same performance as state-of-the-art models like BigGAN-deep [49, 60], as seen in Table 4.

In Table 5, we show that guidance and upsampling improve sample quality along different axes. We use the **u** psampling stack from Nichol and Dhariwal [49] combined with our architecture improvements, which we refer to as ADM-U. While upsampling improves precision while keeping a high recall, guidance provides a knob to trade off diversity for much higher precision. We achieve the best FIDs by using guidance at a lower resolution before upsampling to a higher resolution, indicating that these approaches complement one another.

## **6 Related Work**

Score based generative models were introduced by Song and Ermon [66] as a way of modeling a data distribution using its gradients, and then sampling using Langevin dynamics [73]. Ho et al. [31] found a connection between this method and diffusion models [63], and achieved excellent sample quality by leveraging this connection. After this breakthrough work, many works followed up with

7

Table 5: Comparing our single, upsampling and classifier guided models. The upsamplers are 64 _!_ 256 and 128 _!_ 512. When combining guidance with upsampling, we only guide the lower resolution model. All models are sampled using 250 sampling steps.

more promising results: Kong et al. [36] and Chen et al. [11] demonstrated that diffusion models work well for audio; Jolicoeur-Martineau et al. [32] found that a GAN-like setup could improve samples from these models; Song et al. [67] explored ways to leverage techniques from stochastic differential equations to improve the sample quality obtained by score-based models; Song et al. [64] and Nichol and Dhariwal [49] proposed methods to improve sampling speed; Nichol and Dhariwal [49] and Saharia et al. [60] demonstrated promising results on the difficult ImageNet generation task using upsampling diffusion models. Also related to diffusion models, and following the work of Sohl-Dickstein et al. [63], Goyal et al. [27] described a technique for learning a model with learned iterative generation steps, and found that it could achieve good image samples when trained with a likelihood objective.

Other generative techniques provide natural levers for this trade-off. Brock et al. [8] introduced the truncation trick for GANs, wherein the latent vector is sampled from a truncated normal distribution. They found that increasing truncation naturally led to a decrease in diversity but an increase in fidelity. More recently, Razavi et al. [57] proposed to use classifier rejection sampling to filter out bad samples from an autoregressive likelihood-based model, and found that this technique improved FID. DeVries et al. [16] found that filtering out low-density regions of the training set improves GAN training performance. Most likelihood-based models also allow for low-temperature sampling [1], which provides a natural way to emphasize modes of the data distribution (see Appendix I).

8

Other likelihood-based models have been shown to produce high-fidelity image samples. VQ-VAE [71] and VQ-VAE-2 [57] are autoregressive models trained on top of quantized latent codes, greatly reducing the computational resources required to train these models on large images. These models produce diverse and high quality images, but still fall short of GANs without expensive rejection sampling and special metrics to compensate for blurriness. DCTransformer [48] is a related method which relies on a more intelligent compression scheme. VAEs are another promising class of likelihood-based models, and recent methods such as NVAE [69] and VDVAE [12] have successfully been applied to difficult image generation domains. Energy-based models are another class of likelihood-based models with a rich history [1, 13, 30]. Sampling from the EBM distribution is challenging, and Xie et al. [76] demonstrate that Langevin dynamics can be used to sample coherent images from these models. Du and Mordatch [19] further improve upon this approach, obtaining high quality images. More recently, Gao et al. [24] incorporate diffusion steps into an energy-based model, and find that doing so improves image samples from these models.

body of work [23, 53, 2] aims to optimize GAN latent spaces for text prompts using pre-trained CLIP [55] models. More similar to our work, Song et al. [67] uses a classifier to generate class-conditional CIFAR-10 images with a diffusion model. In some cases, classifiers can act as stand-alone generative models. For example, Santurkar et al. [62] demonstrate that a robust image classifier can be used as a stand-alone generative model, and Grathwohl et al. [28] train a model which is jointly a classifier and an energy-based model.

## **7 Limitations and Future Work**

While we believe diffusion models are an extremely promising direction for generative modeling, they are still slower than GANs at sampling time due to the use of multiple denoising steps (and therefore forward passes). Since our diffusion models are also larger than the competing GAN generators, each forward pass takes anywhere from 5-20 times longer too. A promising direction to reduce this latency gap is Luhman and Luhman [43], who explore a way to distill the DDIM sampling process into a single step model. The samples from the single step model are not yet competitive with GANs, but are much better than previous single-step likelihood-based models. Future work in this direction might be able to completely close the sampling speed gap between diffusion models and GANs without sacrificing image quality.

Unlike GANs, Flows, and VAEs, diffusion models do not learn an explicit latent representation. While DDIM provides a way to encode images into an implicit latent space, it is unclear how semantically meaningful this latent representation is compared to those of other model classes. This could make it difficult to use diffusion models for representation learning or image editing applications.

from the gradients of a classification function. This could be used to condition an image generator with a text caption using a noisy version of CLIP [55], similar to recent methods that guide GANs using text prompts [23, 53, 2]. Our proposed classifier guidance technique is currently limited to labeled datasets. In the future, our method could be extended to unlabeled data by clustering samples to produce synthetic labels [42] or by training discriminative models to use for guidance. This also suggests that large unlabeled datasets could be leveraged in the future to pre-train powerful diffusion models that can later be improved by using a classifier with desirable properties.

## **8 Societal Impact**

Our proposed technique makes generative models more accessible in terms of compute costs, especially because new classifiers can be trained and used on top of existing high-quality diffusion models. While we believe this is generally a benefit of these models, it could also have negative societal implications. For example, cheaper generative models could enable bad actors to generate fake news, propaganda images, or doctored photos. Additionally, the wide-spread deployment of these models could displace jobs in art, graphic design, animation, and photography. One could imagine, however, that democratizing generative models could also have positive impacts in the long run, creating new types of jobs such as generative photo editing. Intentionally deceitful generated

9

images are a more direct concern, and detecting and mitigating propaganda and fake news based on generative models is an ongoing area of research [4, 3, 5].

## **9 Conclusion**

We have shown that diffusion models, a class of likelihood-based models with a stationary training objective, can obtain better sample quality than state-of-the-art GANs. Our improved architecture is sufficient to achieve this on unconditional image generation tasks, and our classifier guidance technique allows us to do so on class-conditional tasks. In the latter case, we find that the scale of the classifier gradients can be adjusted to trade off diversity for fidelity. These guided diffusion models can reduce the sampling time gap between GANs and diffusion models, although diffusion models still require multiple forward passes during sampling. Finally, by combining guidance with upsampling, we can further improve sample quality on high-resolution conditional image synthesis.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
This paper makes a significant contribution by demonstrating that diffusion models can surpass GANs in image synthesis quality on ImageNet across multiple resolutions, a long-standing benchmark challenge. The architectural improvements and classifier guidance method are well-motivated and empirically effective, with strong ablation studies and competitive metrics. The work is timely, technically sound, and has clear impact, as evidenced by its influence on subsequent research in diffusion-based generation.

Overview issues:

- Overreliance on FID without human evaluation undermines perceptual claims: The paper claims superior sample quality primarily based on FID scores, but does not include human perceptual studies to validate that the generated images are indeed more realistic than those from BigGAN-deep. While FID is a standard metric, it is known to imperfectly correlate with human judgment, especially when models trade off diversity for fidelity. This is particularly concerning given that classifier guidance explicitly reduces diversity (as shown in Table 3 and Figure 3), which could lead to overfitting or mode-manipulated samples that score well on FID but appear less natural. A human evaluation, even limited to side-by-side comparisons on a subset of classes, would strengthen the central claim that diffusion models 'beat' GANs in perceptual quality. Without it, the conclusion rests on a potentially flawed proxy.

- Classifier guidance may introduce selection bias not adequately addressed: The paper uses classifier gradients to steer samples toward target classes, but does not analyze whether this introduces systematic biases—such as favoring salient, high-contrast features or overemphasizing classifier-specific artifacts. For instance, if the classifier is more sensitive to background elements or textures than semantic content, guidance could produce images that score well on classification but are visually implausible. The method assumes classifier gradients provide meaningful semantic direction, but this is not verified. Given that noisy-image classifiers are harder to train and may have different failure modes than standard ones, the paper should include diagnostic tests—e.g., visualizing gradient maps or testing robustness across classifier architectures—to ensure guidance improves fidelity without distorting content.

- Computational cost of guidance underreported in real-world terms: While the paper notes that classifier guidance is 'compute-efficient', it does not account for the full inference cost: each sampling step requires both a diffusion model forward pass and a classifier forward pass. At 250 steps, this doubles the computation compared to unconditional sampling. The comparison to BigGAN—where generation is single-pass—is thus misleading in terms of wall-clock time and energy use. The claim of matching BigGAN with 'as few as 25 forward passes' ignores that these are paired with 25 classifier evaluations. For a fair comparison, the paper should report total FLOPs or latency, especially given the known speed limitations of diffusion models. This omission risks overstating practical viability.

- Limited discussion of training stability compared to GANs: One of the paper’s key framing points is that diffusion models avoid GANs’ training instability. However, the paper provides no empirical evidence on training dynamics—no loss curves, no mode collapse observations, no sensitivity analysis to hyperparameters. While diffusion models are theoretically more stable, the improved architectures (with attention, AdaGN, etc.) may introduce new instabilities. A brief analysis of training convergence, variance across runs, or robustness to learning rate changes would strengthen the claim. Given that GANs are often tuned extensively in practice, omitting this comparison leaves open whether the results reflect architectural superiority or simply easier optimization.

- Insufficient justification for gradient scaling hyperparameter: The paper scales classifier gradients by factors up to 10, noting that scale 1.0 produces poor class alignment. However, this scaling lacks principled justification—it effectively sharpens the classifier’s decision boundary in a data-agnostic way. The choice of scale is treated as a free hyperparameter, but no guidance is given on how to select it for new datasets or tasks. Worse, high scales may amplify classifier errors or biases. The paper should either provide a data-driven method for setting the scale (e.g., cross-validation on a validation set) or acknowledge that the results depend critically on a manually tuned, non-robust parameter that limits generalizability.

- Ablation study omits key architectural interactions: The ablation in Table 1 tests components independently, but the final model combines all improvements. This risks missing interaction effects—for example, attention at multiple resolutions may only help when combined with AdaGN or BigGAN blocks. The paper does not test whether the full configuration is strictly better than a subset, nor does it rule out diminishing returns. Given the computational cost of training these models, a more systematic analysis (e.g., factorial design or sequential addition) would strengthen confidence that each component is necessary. As it stands, the claimed 'compounding effect' is not fully substantiated.

- No human evaluation to validate perceptual quality claims: The paper claims diffusion models surpass GANs in sample quality, yet relies entirely on FID and related metrics without human evaluation. While FID is standard, it is known to correlate imperfectly with human judgment—especially when models trade fidelity for diversity, as with classifier guidance. The central claim of superiority rests on this metric, but Figure 4 shows that BigGAN-deep and ADM-G produce samples of comparable visual realism, despite large FID differences. To substantiate the claim that diffusion models 'beat' GANs perceptually, the authors should include a simple human rater study—e.g., a two-alternative forced choice where raters pick the more realistic image between matched BigGAN-deep and ADM-G samples across several classes. Without this, the paper risks equating metric gains with perceptual progress.

- Missing analysis of classifier guidance failure modes: Classifier guidance depends on the assumption that classifier gradients provide meaningful semantic direction, but the paper does not test where this assumption breaks. For example, gradients might amplify spurious correlations—such as backgrounds or watermarks—leading to samples that score well on FID but are semantically invalid. The authors should analyze failure cases by visualizing gradient maps for misclassified or low-quality samples, and test robustness by training multiple classifiers with different architectures or data augmentations. Ablating the classifier’s training procedure—e.g., using random crops or different noise schedules—would reveal whether guidance is sensitive to classifier artifacts. This is critical because the method’s success hinges on an unstudied inductive bias in the classifier’s learned features.

- No comparison of sampling efficiency in wall-clock terms: The paper claims classifier guidance enables performance close to BigGAN-deep with 'as few as 25 forward passes', but does not report wall-clock sampling time or compute cost. Each step in ADM-G requires both a diffusion model and a classifier evaluation, making it significantly more expensive per pass than BigGAN-deep’s single forward pass. To assess practical viability, the authors should report end-to-end sampling latency on identical hardware, or total FLOPs per sample. A table comparing inference time, memory, and energy use between BigGAN-deep and ADM-G at matched FID would ground the efficiency claims. Without this, the narrative of competitive sampling speed is misleading, especially for real-world deployment.

- Lack of ablation for combined architectural components: The paper introduces multiple architectural changes—attention at multiple resolutions, AdaGN, BigGAN blocks, multi-head attention—but only evaluates them additively in Table 1. It does not test whether these components interact synergistically or if some are redundant when others are present. For example, AdaGN might only help when combined with class conditioning, or multi-resolution attention may only matter with sufficient depth. The authors should report results for the full model versus a minimal configuration that includes only the highest-impact components identified in Table 1 and 2. A sequential ablation—adding one component at a time to a baseline—would clarify whether gains truly compound or plateau, strengthening confidence in the final architecture design.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Include human perceptual evaluation to validate that lower FID scores correspond to higher visual quality compared to BigGAN-deep

- Analyze and report the total computational cost (FLOPs or latency) of classifier-guided sampling, including both diffusion and classifier forward passes

- Provide a principled method or sensitivity analysis for selecting the classifier gradient scale, rather than relying on manual tuning

- Add discussion or empirical evidence on training stability, including convergence behavior and hyperparameter sensitivity compared to GANs

- Expand ablation study to test interaction effects among architectural components, justifying the necessity of the full configuration

comments.title: FID values for upsampling models contradict improvement claim
comments.quote: We achieve an FID of 2.97 on ImageNet  $128 \times 128$ , 4.59 on ImageNet  $256 \times 256$ , and 7.72 on ImageNet  $512 \times 512$ , and we match BigGAN-deep even with as few as 25 forward passes per sample, all while maintaining better coverage of the distribution. Finally, we find that classifier guidance combines well with upsampling diffusion models, further improving FID to 3.94 on ImageNet  $256 \times 256$  and 3.85 on ImageNet  $512 \times 512$ .
comments.explanation: The abstract states that upsampling 'further improves' FID to 3.94 on ImageNet 256×256, but this value is higher than the 4.59 reported for the base guided model, meaning performance is worse, not better. A lower FID indicates superior quality, so describing 3.94 as an improvement over 4.59 is incorrect. This suggests either a typo in the numbers or a misstatement about the direction of improvement. The sentence must be corrected to reflect actual performance gains or clarify that the baseline differs.

comments.title: FID values in ablation table presented as deltas without clarification
comments.explanation: Table 1 reports FID changes as differences from a baseline (15.33 at 700K, 13.21 at 1200K), but this is not stated in the caption or text. Entries like '-0.21' are easily misinterpreted as absolute scores near zero rather than relative improvements. This violates standard reporting practices and risks serious misunderstanding, especially for replication. The table should present absolute FID values or include a clear 'ΔFID' column with a footnote explaining the baseline.

comments.title: Claim about depth dominating training cost lacks supporting data
comments.quote: On wall-clock (Figure 5, Appendix A.1) we find that increased depth hurts training time most, so we opt not to use this change in further experiments.
comments.explanation: The assertion that increased depth 'hurts training time most' implies a comparative analysis across architectural changes, but no timing data are provided for width, attention, or BigGAN blocks—components that also increase compute. Without side-by-side per-iteration measurements under identical conditions, the claim that depth has the largest impact is unsupported. The conclusion affects model design choices and should be backed by quantitative evidence or rephrased to reflect only that depth increases training time.

comments.title: Ambiguous operator in AdaGN definition
comments.quote: We define this layer as  $\mathrm{AdaGN}(h,y) = y_s$  GroupNorm  $(h) + y_b$ , where  $h$  is the intermediate activations of the residual block following the first convolution, and  $y = [y_s,y_b]$  is obtained from a linear projection of the timestep and class embedding.
comments.explanation: The expression $\mathrm{AdaGN}(h,y) = y_s$ GroupNorm $(h) + y_b$ lacks an explicit multiplication operator between $y_s$ and GroupNorm$(h)$. Since $y_s$ is a vector and GroupNorm$(h)$ is a tensor, the intended operation is channel-wise scaling, which should be written as $y_s \cdot \text{GroupNorm}(h) + y_b$. Juxtaposition without an operator is ambiguous and formally incorrect in mathematical notation, risking misinterpretation.

comments.title: Final model omits clarification on residual rescaling
comments.quote: In the rest of the paper, we use this final improved model architecture as our default: variable width with 2 residual blocks per resolution, multiple heads with 64 channels per head, attention at 32, 16 and 8 resolutions, BigGAN residual blocks for up and downsampling, and adaptive group normalization for injecting timestep and class embeddings into residual blocks.
comments.explanation: The final architecture description includes components that were ablated (e.g., BigGAN blocks, AdaGN) but does not specify whether residual rescaling ($\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$) is used, despite being tested in Table 1 and found to degrade FID (ΔFID +0.16). The omission creates ambiguity about the exact configuration. Given that it harms performance, the paper should explicitly state whether it is excluded in the final model.

comments.title: Gradient scaling lacks proper probabilistic justification
comments.quote: To understand the effect of scaling classifier gradients, note that  $s \cdot \nabla_x \log p(y|x) = \nabla_x \log \frac{1}{Z} p(y|x)^s$ , where  $Z$  is an arbitrary constant. As a result, the conditioning process is still theoretically grounded in a re-normalized classifier distribution proportional to  $p(y|x)^s$ .
comments.explanation: While the algebraic identity holds, interpreting $p(y|x)^s$ as defining a valid sampling distribution requires that $\int p(y|x)^s p(x) dx$ be normalizable, which is not guaranteed. The classifier $p_\phi(y|x)$ is trained on noisy data and does not form a proper joint model with $p(x)$. Thus, the guidance process does not correspond to exact conditioning on a consistent posterior. The mechanism remains heuristic, not theoretically grounded in a re-normalized distribution.

comments.title: Classifier notation contradicts t-conditioning requirement
comments.quote:
peg)

$p_{\phi}(y|x_t,t) = p_{\phi}(y|x_t)$  and  $\epsilon_{\theta}(x_t,t) = \epsilon_{\theta}(x_t)$  for brevity, noting that they refer to separate functions for each timestep  $t$  and at training time the models must be conditioned on the
comments.explanation: The notation $p_\phi(y|x_t,t) = p_\phi(y|x_t)$ implies functional independence from $t$, yet the text insists the model 'must be conditioned on the input $t$'. This is contradictory: if $t$ is not an input, the classifier cannot adapt to noise level. Since $x_t$'s noise varies with $t$, omitting $t$-conditioning risks learning noise-correlated features. The implementation must explicitly include $t$-embedding to align with the theoretical derivation.

comments.title: Claim of guided unconditional model approaching conditional FID is overstated
comments.quote: We see that, with a high enough scale, the guided unconditional model can get quite close to the FID of an unguided conditional model, although training directly with the class labels still helps.
comments.explanation: The guided unconditional model achieves FID 12.00, while the unguided conditional model achieves 10.94—a gap of 1.06, which is meaningful in FID terms. The phrase 'get quite close' overstates the result, especially given the larger sFID gap (10.40 vs 6.02). The data show guidance improves unconditional models, but not that they approach conditional ones in a strong sense. The claim should be revised to reflect the actual performance gap.

comments.title: Precision-recall trade-off masks potential mode collapse
comments.quote: We explicitly evaluate how this trade-off varies with the gradient scale in Figure 3. We see that scaling the gradients beyond 1.0 smoothly trades off recall (a measure of diversity) for higher precision and IS (measures of fidelity).
comments.explanation: The trade-off is described as 'smooth', but Table 3 shows a sharp drop in recall—from 0.63 to 0.32 (49% decrease) at scale 10.0—while IS increases by 181%. Such extreme changes suggest a non-smooth transition and raise concerns about mode collapse, as IS rewards class purity without penalizing lack of variety. The paper should acknowledge this risk and discuss whether high-scale samples exhibit reduced variation.

comments.title: No justification for fixed gradient scale across timesteps
comments.quote:
for all  $t$  from  $T$  to 1 do
$\mu ,\Sigma \leftarrow \mu_{\theta}(x_t),\Sigma_{\theta}(x_t)$ $x_{t - 1}\gets$  sample from  $\mathcal{N}(\mu +s\Sigma \nabla_{x_t}\log p_\phi (y|x_t),\Sigma)$
comments.explanation: Algorithm 1 applies the same gradient scale $s$ at every timestep, despite varying signal-to-noise ratios. Early steps (high noise) may benefit less from guidance, while later steps (low noise) may require smaller $s$ to avoid overshooting. The paper provides no ablation or theoretical justification for a constant $s$. A time-varying scale $s_t$ could offer better control, and the fixed choice should be acknowledged as a simplifying assumption.

comments.title: Classifier trained on noisy images lacks cross-t evaluation
comments.quote: We train these classifiers on the same noising distribution as the corresponding diffusion model, and also add random crops to reduce overfitting.
comments.explanation: The classifier is trained on noisy images but not evaluated on clean or intermediate-noise samples. Its accuracy may vary significantly across $t$, affecting gradient reliability during sampling. Without reporting per-timestep performance, it's unclear whether $\nabla_{x_t} \log p_\phi(y|x_t)$ is trustworthy throughout the process. The paper should include such analysis to validate gradient quality.

comments.title: Claim of substantially outperforming GANs overstated at 512×512
comments.quote: For higher resolution ImageNet, we observe that classifier guidance allows our models to substantially outperform the best GANs.
comments.explanation: On ImageNet 512×512, ADM-G achieves FID 7.72 vs BigGAN-deep's 8.43—a 0.71 improvement, which is meaningful but not 'substantial' in context. Moreover, recall drops from 0.29 to 0.42, indicating reduced diversity. The term 'substantially outperform' overstates the gain, especially given the trade-off. The claim should reflect that FID improves modestly at a cost in coverage.

comments.title: 25-step sampling does not match BigGAN-deep in quality
comments.quote: These models obtain perceptual quality similar to GANs, while maintaining a higher coverage of the distribution as measured by recall, and can even do so using only 25 sampling steps.
comments.explanation: For ImageNet 512×512, ADM-G (25 steps) has FID 8.41—slightly worse than BigGAN-deep's 8.43—while recall is higher (0.47 vs 0.29). The claim of 'similar perceptual quality' is barely supported, and 'maintaining higher coverage' ignores the FID deficit. The full 250-step model achieves 7.72, showing the 25-step version is significantly weaker. The statement should clarify that 25-step sampling approaches but does not match BigGAN-deep.

comments.title: Inconsistent model naming with undefined 'ADM (dropout)'
comments.quote: ADM refers to our ablated diffusion model, and ADM-G additionally uses classifier guidance.
comments.explanation: The text defines ADM and ADM-G, but Table 4 includes 'ADM (dropout)'—a better-performing variant (e.g., 2.07 vs 2.61 on ImageNet 64×64)—without explanation. This creates confusion: is this the final model? If so, the claim that ADM sets the state-of-the-art is inaccurate. The paper must define 'ADM (dropout)' and clarify its role in the results.

comments.title: Misattribution of score-based model origin
comments.quote: Score based generative models were introduced by Song and Ermon [66] as a way of modeling a data distribution using its gradients, and then sampling using Langevin dynamics [73].
comments.explanation: This misattributes the origin of score-based models. While Song and Ermon (2019) advanced the framework, the core ideas—score matching (Hyvärinen, 2005) and Langevin sampling (Welling et al., 2011)—predate their work. The paper should credit Song and Ermon with a key advancement, not the introduction, to accurately reflect the field's history.

comments.title: Overstates novelty of diversity-fidelity trade-off
comments.quote: One missing element from previous work on diffusion models is a way to trade off diversity for fidelity.
comments.explanation: This claim is incorrect. Nichol and Dhariwal (2021) already introduced mechanisms like dynamic thresholding and noise conditioning to control fidelity and diversity in diffusion models. Additionally, rejection sampling and temperature scaling in VQ-VAE-2 provided such trade-offs earlier. The paper should acknowledge these prior methods and position classifier guidance as a scaling and refinement, not a conceptual novelty.

comments.title: Understates prior work on classifier guidance
comments.quote: More similar to our work, Song et al. *[67]* uses a classifier to generate class-conditional CIFAR-10 images with a diffusion model.
comments.explanation: Song et al. (2021) did more than use classifier guidance—they introduced it as a core method for steering diffusion sampling using classifier gradients, the same mechanism used here. While this paper scales it to ImageNet and combines it with upsampling, the foundational technique is not new. The related work should clearly frame the contribution as architectural and scaling progress, not conceptual innovation.

comments.title: Misleading claim about reducing sampling time gap
comments.quote: These guided diffusion models can reduce the sampling time gap between GANs and diffusion models, although diffusion models still require multiple forward passes during sampling.
comments.explanation: Classifier guidance does not reduce the sampling time gap—in fact, it widens it. Each step requires both a diffusion and a classifier forward pass, doubling the computation. The paper notes performance with 'as few as 25 forward passes', but ignores that each involves two evaluations. The claim incorrectly implies efficiency gains. It should instead state that fewer steps are needed for high quality, despite higher per-step cost.

comments.title: Overstates sufficiency of architecture for beating GANs
comments.quote: Our improved architecture is sufficient to achieve this on unconditional image generation tasks, and our classifier guidance technique allows us to do so on class-conditional tasks.
comments.explanation: The best FID scores (e.g., 2.97 on 128×128) are achieved using classifier guidance, a conditional method. The paper does not report an unconditional FID that beats BigGAN-deep. Thus, claiming the architecture alone is 'sufficient' to surpass GANs overreaches—the evidence shows guidance is essential for the central claim. The statement should be revised to reflect that architecture enables gains when combined with guidance.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
This paper presents significant improvements to diffusion models for image synthesis, introducing architectural enhancements and classifier guidance that achieve state-of-the-art FID scores on ImageNet at multiple resolutions. While the empirical results are impressive, several methodological concerns need addressing regarding the evaluation methodology, computational comparisons, and the characterization of diversity-fidelity trade-offs.

The paper makes a strong contribution by demonstrating that diffusion models can surpass GANs in sample quality through architectural improvements and classifier guidance. The ablation studies are thorough and the results are compelling. However, the evaluation relies heavily on FID without sufficient discussion of its limitations, computational comparisons to GANs are incomplete, and the diversity implications of classifier guidance require more thorough analysis.

Overview issues:

- Insufficient Analysis of Diversity Loss with Classifier Guidance: The paper shows that classifier guidance improves FID by trading off diversity for fidelity (Table 3, Figure 3), but this trade-off is not adequately characterized. While Precision/Recall metrics are provided, the paper does not analyze whether certain classes or modes are disproportionately affected by guidance. The visual examples in Figure 2 show dramatic improvements for a specific class, but there's no systematic analysis of whether guidance causes mode collapse or reduces intra-class diversity across the dataset. This is particularly important given that the paper claims to maintain "better coverage of the distribution" than GANs, yet Table 3 shows recall dropping from 0.63 to 0.52 with guidance on ImageNet 256×256.

- Incomplete Computational Cost Analysis: The paper claims competitive computational requirements (Section 5, Table 10 in Appendix B) but the analysis is insufficient. While training compute is compared to BigGAN-deep, the paper does not provide wall-clock time comparisons for sampling, which is crucial since diffusion models require multiple forward passes. The paper mentions 5-20× longer per forward pass and 250-1000 sampling steps, but no concrete benchmarks are provided against GAN sampling times. This makes it difficult to assess the practical feasibility of the approach, especially for the claim that ADM-G with 25 steps "matches BigGAN-deep" - matching in FID does not equal matching in computational efficiency.

- Over-reliance on FID as Primary Evaluation Metric: The paper uses FID as the default metric throughout, acknowledging in Section 2 that these metrics "do not fully capture diversity" and that "finding better metrics for sample quality evaluation is still an open problem." However, the claims of superiority are based almost exclusively on FID improvements. The paper includes Precision/Recall and IS, but these are not integrated into the main narrative or used to qualify the FID results. This is particularly problematic when comparing to GANs, as different model architectures may optimize for different aspects of image quality that FID may not capture equally well.

- Limited Statistical Significance Reporting: The paper reports FID scores without confidence intervals or statistical significance testing. Given that FID can vary based on random seeds and implementation details (as noted in Section 2 regarding re-computing metrics), the lack of statistical rigor makes it difficult to assess whether reported improvements are significant. This is especially important for the claimed state-of-the-art results where differences may be small (e.g., 2.57 vs 3.84 FID on LSUN Horses). The paper should include multiple runs with confidence intervals to establish the reliability of the results.

- Unjustified Extrapolation of Architectural Improvements: The architectural improvements are ablated on ImageNet 128×128 (Section 3, Table 1) but then applied to higher resolutions (256×256 and 512×512) without validation. The paper assumes these improvements generalize, but different resolutions may require different architectural choices. No ablation is provided for higher resolutions to confirm that the same architecture remains optimal. This is a significant gap given that the main results claim state-of-the-art performance at these higher resolutions.

- Missing demonstration of non-vacuous classifier guidance: The paper introduces classifier guidance but lacks a concrete demonstration showing when and why this technique fails. To establish that the result has bite, the authors should include a negative example: train a classifier on a dataset with noisy or ambiguous labels, then show that guidance either fails to improve sample quality or actively degrades it. This would demonstrate the boundaries of when classifier guidance is appropriate and validate the claim that it works by 'focusing on modes' rather than being a universal improvement.

- No worked example of architecture improvements: The architectural ablations in Table 1 are presented as incremental changes, but the paper never shows the complete final architecture specification. A complete paper should include a fully specified model diagram or pseudocode showing the exact architecture used for state-of-the-art results, including channel counts at each resolution, attention head configurations, and skip connections. This would allow readers to understand and reproduce the final model without having to infer the complete architecture from the ablation table.

- Undeveloped comparison to BigGAN truncation trade-off: The paper claims classifier guidance is 'strictly better than BigGAN-deep when trading off FID for Inception Score' but this comparison is underdeveloped. The paper should compute the full precision-recall curve for both methods at multiple guidance scales and truncation levels, showing exactly where each method dominates. Currently, Figure 6 in Appendix A.2 is referenced but not shown in the main text, making it difficult to assess the validity of this claim that is central to positioning the work against GANs.

- Missing implementation guidance for high-resolution sampling: While the paper mentions combining guidance with upsampling achieves better FID, it provides no practical guidance on how to implement this combination effectively. The paper should specify the exact guidance scales used at each resolution in the two-stage pipeline, and analyze whether different scales are optimal for different resolutions. Without this implementation detail, readers cannot reproduce the best reported results (FID 3.85 on ImageNet 512×512) or understand how the trade-offs change across the pipeline.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Provide comprehensive analysis of diversity loss from classifier guidance, including class-wise diversity metrics and intra-class variation studies

- Add detailed computational benchmarks including wall-clock sampling times and FLOPs comparisons with GAN baselines

- Include statistical significance testing for all reported metrics with confidence intervals from multiple runs

- Validate architectural choices on higher resolutions with proper ablation studies

- Integrate Precision/Recall and IS metrics more thoroughly into the evaluation narrative to balance FID-centric claims

comments.title: Table 1 contains impossible negative FID values
comments.explanation: FID is a distance metric that cannot be negative. The negative values in Table 1 are mathematically impossible for absolute FID scores. The first row shows plausible absolute FID values (15.33, 13.21), while the negative entries appear to be relative improvements (deltas) from the baseline. Rewrite Table 1 to show absolute FID values by adding the deltas to the baseline FID of 15.33 at 700K and 13.21 at 1200K, or clearly label the negative entries as relative improvements with the baseline reference.

comments.title: Table 2 shows impossible negative FID values
comments.quote:
Table 2: Ablation of attention heads. More heads or lower channels per heads both improve FID. The base model was a smaller version of the best model from Table 1.

|  Number of heads | Channels per head | FID  |
| --- | --- | --- |
|  1 |  | 14.08  |
|  2 |  | -0.50  |
|  4 |  | -0.97  |
|  8 |  | -1.17  |
|   | 32 | -1.36  |
|   | 64 | -1.03  |
|   | 128 | -1.08  |
comments.explanation: FID is a distance metric that cannot be negative. The negative values in Table 2 are mathematically impossible for absolute FID scores. The first row shows a plausible absolute FID of 14.08, while subsequent rows show negative values that appear to be relative improvements. Since the text states these are ablation results, the negative entries must be deltas relative to the baseline FID of 14.08. Rewrite the table to show absolute FID scores by adding the deltas to 14.08, or add a clear note that the values represent relative improvements over the baseline.

comments.title: Missing gradient scale factor in Algorithm 2
comments.quote:
Algorithm 2 Classifier guided DDIM sampling, given a diffusion model  $\epsilon_{\theta}(x_t)$ , classifier  $p_{\phi}(y|x_t)$  and gradient scale  $s$ .
Input: class label  $y$  gradient scale s
$x_{T}\gets$  sample from  $\mathcal{N}(0,\mathbf{I})$
for all  $t$  from  $T$  to 1 do
$\hat{\epsilon}\gets \epsilon_{\theta}(x_t) - \sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_t}\nabla_{x_t}\log p_\phi (y|x_t)$ $x_{t - 1}\leftarrow \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_{t - 1}}\left(\frac{x_t - \sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_t}\epsilon}{\sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}}\right) + \sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_{t - 1}}\hat{\epsilon}$
end for
return  $x_0$
comments.explanation: Algorithm 2 is missing the gradient scale factor $s$ that is listed in the input parameters. The algorithm description explicitly mentions 'gradient scale s' as an input, and the paper's experimental section discusses varying this scale parameter (Table 3 shows results for scale 1.0 and 10.0). Algorithm 1 correctly incorporates the scale as $s\Sigma \nabla_{x_t}\log p_\phi(y|x_t)$. Rewrite the line as '$\hat{\epsilon}\gets \epsilon_{\theta}(x_t) - s\sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_t}\nabla_{x_t}\log p_\phi (y|x_t)$' to include the gradient scale factor $s$ as specified in the algorithm input.

comments.title: Missing classifier timestep conditioning in algorithms
comments.quote:
Algorithm 1 Classifier guided diffusion sampling, given a diffusion model  $(\mu_{\theta}(x_t),\Sigma_{\theta}(x_t))$  , classifier  $p_{\phi}(y|x_t)$  , and gradient scale s.
Input: class label  $y$  gradient scale s
$x_{T}\gets$  sample from  $\mathcal{N}(0,\mathbf{I})$
for all  $t$  from  $T$  to 1 do
$\mu ,\Sigma \leftarrow \mu_{\theta}(x_t),\Sigma_{\theta}(x_t)$ $x_{t - 1}\gets$  sample from  $\mathcal{N}(\mu +s\Sigma \nabla_{x_t}\log p_\phi (y|x_t),\Sigma)$

comments.explanation: The algorithms omit timestep conditioning for the classifier, despite the text stating 'we train a classifier $p_{\phi}(y|x_t,t)$ on noisy images $x_t$' and noting that '$p_{\phi}(y|x_t,t) = p_{\phi}(y|x_t)$ for brevity'. The classifier gradients should be computed as $\nabla_{x_t}\log p_{\phi}(y|x_t,t)$ with the timestep $t$ as an additional input to the classifier. This is crucial because the classifier is trained on differently noised images at each timestep, and the gradient computation must match the training distribution. Add timestep conditioning by changing the gradient computation to '$\nabla_{x_t}\log p_\phi (y|x_t,t)$' in both algorithms.

comments.title: Contradiction between recall claim and table data

For ImageNet 256×256, ADM-G has recall 0.52, also lower than ADM's 0.63. For ImageNet 512×512, ADM-G has recall 0.42, again lower than ADM's 0.60. The guided models consistently have lower recall than unguided models, which contradicts the claim of 'maintaining a higher coverage'. The text should state that guidance trades off recall for higher precision. Rewrite as: 'These models obtain perceptual quality similar to GANs, while trading off some coverage of the distribution (lower recall) for higher precision.'

the variance $\Sigma_{\theta}(x_{t},t)$ to a constant as done in Ho et al. *[31]* is sub-optimal for sampling with fewer diffusion steps, and propose to parameterize $\Sigma_{\theta}(x_{t},t)$ as a neural network whose output $v$ is interpolated as $\Sigma_{\theta}(x_{t},t)=\exp(v\log\beta_{t}+(1-v)\log\tilde{\beta}_{t})$.
comments.explanation: The notation is inconsistent. The paper uses $\Sigma_{\theta}$ which typically denotes a covariance matrix, but the interpolation formula $\exp(v\log\beta_{t}+(1-v)\log\tilde{\beta}_{t})$ produces a scalar value. This simplifies to $\beta_{t}^{v} \cdot \tilde{\beta}_{t}^{1-v}$, representing geometric interpolation between scalar bounds. Nichol and Dhariwal's original formulation uses $\log \sigma_{t}^{2} = v \log \beta_{t} + (1-v) \log \tilde{\beta}_{t}$ for scalar variance. Rewrite the quoted text as: 'Nichol and Dhariwal *[49]* find that fixing the variance $\sigma_{\theta}^{2}(x_{t},t)$ to a constant as done in Ho et al. *[31]* is sub-optimal for sampling with fewer diffusion steps, and propose to parameterize $\sigma_{\theta}^{2}(x_{t},t)$ as a neural network whose output $v$ is interpolated as $\log \sigma_{\theta}^{2}(x_{t},t) = v\log\beta_{t} + (1-v)\log\tilde{\beta}_{t}$' because this matches the original formulation and avoids notational inconsistency between matrix and scalar variance.

Nichol and Dhariwal's paper defines the hybrid objective as $L_{\text{simple}} + \lambda L_{\text{vlb}}$, where $L_{\text{vlb}}$ is the variational lower bound. The term $L_{\text{vib}}$ is not defined in the diffusion literature and likely resulted from a transcription error. Rewrite the quoted text as: 'Additionally, Nichol and Dhariwal *[49]* propose a hybrid objective for training both $\epsilon_{\theta}(x_{t},t)$ and $\sigma_{\theta}^{2}(x_{t},t)$ using the weighted sum $L_{\text{simple}}+\lambda L_{\text{vlb}}$' because this corrects both the objective notation and maintains consistency with the scalar variance parameterization.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
This paper presents architectural improvements to diffusion models and introduces classifier guidance to trade diversity for fidelity. The empirical results on ImageNet are strong and establish diffusion models as competitive with GANs on standard metrics. However, the theoretical justification for guidance scaling remains heuristic, and the heavy dependency on pre-trained classifiers introduces practical fragility that the current analysis does not fully address.

The paper introduces meaningful architectural refinements to diffusion models and demonstrates that classifier guidance can effectively trade diversity for fidelity. The empirical results on ImageNet are strong, establishing diffusion models as competitive with GANs on standard metrics. However, the theoretical justification for guidance scaling remains heuristic, and the heavy dependency on pre-trained classifiers introduces practical fragility that the current analysis does not fully address.

Overview issues:

- Theoretical justification for gradient scaling in classifier guidance: Section 4 introduces classifier guidance by scaling the classifier gradient by a factor $s$ and adding it to the reverse diffusion mean. The authors justify this by noting it corresponds to re-normalizing the classifier distribution to $p(y|x)^s$. This reasoning holds for the score itself, but the reverse diffusion step relies on an approximation of the true posterior $p_	heta(x_{t-1}|x_t)$. When $s$ becomes large, the guidance term dominates the noise prediction, pushing the sampler into regions where the diffusion model's Gaussian approximation is likely invalid. The paper does not analyze the error introduced by this approximation or provide bounds on how large $s$ can be before the sampling process diverges from the intended distribution. Readers will wonder whether the high-fidelity samples at $s=10$ are actually sampling from a coherent distribution or just optimizing a local classifier score. Adding a theoretical analysis of the guidance's impact on the reverse process variance or a sensitivity analysis of $s$ on sampling stability would resolve this.

- Practical fragility due to classifier dependency: The guidance method requires a pre-trained classifier $p_ i(y|x_t)$ trained on noisy images. Table 3 demonstrates that sample quality fluctuates dramatically with the guidance scale, and the method implicitly assumes the classifier provides accurate gradients across the entire sampling trajectory. If the classifier overfits or misclassifies noisy latents, the guidance will steer the diffusion process toward artifacts or mode collapse. The limitations section acknowledges this dependency but stops short of empirical investigation. The paper would benefit from a detailed ablation showing how classifier accuracy, training data size, or overfitting directly impacts the precision-recall trade-off. Without this, the method appears brittle in practice, as its success is tightly coupled to an external model that may not generalize across datasets or resolutions.

- Ambiguous presentation of architecture ablations: Table 1 reports architecture changes using delta values rather than absolute FID scores. The baseline configuration yields an FID of 13.21 at 1200K iterations, but subsequent rows list improvements as negative deltas (e.g., -0.21, -0.48, -0.54). It is unclear whether these deltas accumulate sequentially or apply independently to the baseline. The final row shows a sharp drop to -3.14, which likely represents the full combined configuration, but the table layout obscures whether the improvements are strictly additive or if certain changes interact synergistically. This formatting choice forces readers to mentally reconstruct the ablation matrix, increasing the risk of misinterpretation. Replacing the delta notation with absolute FID values for each configuration would eliminate ambiguity and allow direct verification of the compounding effects.

- Metric reliance masks severe diversity loss: The paper uses FID, sFID, IS, Precision, and Recall to claim superiority over GANs. Table 3 reveals that applying classifier guidance at scale 10.0 boosts Precision to 0.88 but crashes Recall to 0.32. FID balances these two metrics, so a high FID score can coexist with extreme mode collapse. The authors frame the precision-recall trade-off as a desirable feature, yet they do not quantify how much diversity loss is acceptable before the model becomes practically useless. Relying solely on FID and sFID obscures this degradation, especially since GANs are already known to suffer from low recall. Including a human evaluation study or analyzing the precision-recall curve across multiple guidance scales would provide a more honest assessment of sample quality and prevent metric gaming.

- Unsubstantiated compute and efficiency claims: The abstract and results sections assert that the proposed method is compute-efficient and can match BigGAN-deep with as few as 25 forward passes. However, the main text never reports the actual training compute (GPU hours or FLOPs) required to train these models. Table 10 in Appendix B supposedly contains the compute breakdown, but relegating it to the appendix weakens the central claim of efficiency. Diffusion models typically demand significantly more training compute than GANs due to their iterative denoising objective, and the paper does not contextualize the 25-step sampling advantage against the higher upfront training cost. Moving the compute analysis to the main text and explicitly comparing training FLOPs alongside sampling steps would ground the efficiency claims and allow a fair comparison with GAN baselines.

Recommendation: Major revision. The theoretical grounding for guidance scaling is incomplete, the heavy reliance on an external classifier lacks robustness analysis, and the compute claims are buried in an appendix. These gaps prevent a fair assessment of the method's practical viability and theoretical soundness, requiring substantial clarification and additional analysis before publication.

Revision targets:

- Provide a theoretical analysis or error bounds for the gradient scaling factor $s$ in classifier guidance, explaining its impact on the reverse diffusion approximation.

- Include an empirical sensitivity analysis showing how classifier accuracy, overfitting, or training data size affects guidance quality and sample diversity.

- Replace the delta notation in Table 1 with absolute FID values for each ablation configuration to clarify cumulative and interactive effects.

- Move the compute and training FLOP analysis from Appendix B to the main text, and explicitly compare training costs alongside sampling steps to substantiate efficiency claims.

periments.

Song et al. *[64]* propose DDIM, which formulates an alternative non-Markovian noising process that has the same forward marginals as DDPM,
comments.explanation: The text incorrectly describes DDIM's forward process as non-Markovian. DDIM preserves the exact Markovian forward chain of DDPM; its innovation lies in defining a deterministic, non-Markovian reverse process by zeroing out the reverse noise variance. Clarifying this distinction prevents conceptual confusion about the model's generative trajectory.

They also note that training with this objective and using their corresponding sampling procedure is equivalent to the denoising score matching model from Song and Ermon *[65]*, who use Langevin dynamics to sample from a denoising model trained with multiple noise levels to produce high quality image samples. We often use “di
comments.explanation: Equating the DDPM sampling procedure with Song and Ermon's Langevin dynamics is imprecise. DDPM relies on a learned Markovian reverse chain with fixed or learned variances, whereas Langevin dynamics requires iterative external updates with a fixed step size. Distinguishing between the learned reverse diffusion chain and external Langevin updates will align the background section with standard terminology.

The channels-depth product increases by 60%, and parameter count grows by roughly 28%. This directly contradicts the claim that model size is held relatively constant. comments.title: Table 2 inconsistently mixes absolute and delta FID values

comments.title: Inaccurate claim about sFID dominance in Table 4
comments.quote: Our diffusion models can obtain the best FID on each task, and the best sFID on all but one task.
comments.explanation: The data in Table 4 contradicts this statement. The proposed models achieve the lowest sFID on only five of the seven tasks. They fall short on LSUN Cats (StyleGAN2: 6.33 vs ADM dropout: 6.69) and ImageNet 64x64 (IDDPM: 3.79 vs ADM: 3.77).
