comments.title: Missing upsampling results in Table 4 reference
comments.quote:
his greatly improves FID on ImageNet  $256 \times 256$ , but does not reach the same performance as state-of-the-art models like BigGAN-deep [49, 60], as seen in Table 4.

In T
comments.explanation: The text claims that the performance of the two-stage upsampling approach can be seen in Table 4, but the table lacks a row for this specific method. It lists VQ-GAN (RS), which uses rejection sampling, not the upsampling stack described in the paragraph. The misplaced citations [49, 60] next to BigGAN-deep add to the confusion. The authors should either add a dedicated row for the upsampling stack results or remove the reference to Table 4.

comments.title: Missing element claim contradicts low-temperature sampling
comments.quote: One missing element from previous work on diffusion models is a way to trade off diversity for fidelity.
comments.explanation: This assertion is directly contradicted a few sentences later when the authors acknowledge that likelihood-based models support low-temperature sampling to emphasize data modes. While classifier guidance introduces a distinct mechanism for the precision-recall trade-off, framing it as a completely missing capability ignores established practices. The positioning would be more precise if the authors clarified that temperature scaling exists but argue why classifier guidance provides a more effective trade-off for diffusion models.

comments.title: Conflation of step count with wall-clock sampling time
comments.quote: These guided diffusion models can reduce the sampling time gap between GANs and diffusion models, although diffusion models still require multiple forward passes during sampling.
comments.explanation: The conclusion claims that guided diffusion models reduce the sampling time gap, but the paper only provides evidence regarding step counts. Because diffusion sampling is strictly sequential while GAN inference is a single parallel operation, reducing the number of steps does not automatically reduce wall-clock time without accounting for per-step latency. The conclusion overstates the practical speed advantage by equating algorithmic step efficiency with actual sampling duration. The authors should explicitly distinguish between step reduction and time reduction.

comments.title: Overgeneralization regarding sampling speed of likelihood-based models
comments.quote: Furthermore, except for VAEs, sampling from these models is slower than GANs in terms of wall-clock time.
comments.explanation: This statement inaccurately groups all likelihood-based models together. Normalizing flows and many modern autoregressive models sample in a single pass or with highly parallelizable decoding, making their wall-clock sampling times comparable to or faster than GANs. Distinguishing between iterative and single-pass likelihood models will prevent mischaracterizing a significant branch of prior work.

comments.title: Typographical error in gradient subscript
comments.quote: and then use gradients  $\nabla_{x_i}\log p_{\phi}(y|x_t,t)$  to guide the diffusion sampling process towards an arbitrary class label  $y$
comments.explanation: The subscript $i$ in $\nabla_{x_i}$ is a typo. It should be $t$ to correctly denote the gradient with respect to the noisy image $x_t$, which matches the notation used throughout the rest of the section and in both algorithms.

comments.title: Overstated per-pass latency without empirical support
comments.quote: Since our diffusion models are also larger than the competing GAN generators, each forward pass takes anywhere from 5-20 times longer too.
comments.explanation: The claim that each forward pass is 5-20 times slower lacks empirical justification in the text. The paper reports model sizes around 180 million parameters, which are comparable to BigGAN-deep. Given similar parameter counts, a 5-20x difference in wall-clock time per pass is unexpected. The authors should either provide benchmark timings to substantiate this range or revise the statement to reflect that latency depends on implementation details and hardware.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of DDIM as a latent representation
comments.quote: While DDIM provides a way to encode images into an implicit latent space, it is unclear how semantically meaningful this latent representation is compared to those of other model classes.
comments.explanation: DDIM inversion does not produce a learned latent representation suitable for representation learning or editing. It merely provides a deterministic coordinate transformation along the diffusion trajectory. Treating this trajectory coordinate as a latent space conflates sampling paths with learned feature embeddings. Clarifying that DDIM offers a deterministic inversion path rather than a semantically structured latent space will prevent confusion.

comments.title: Imprecise description of classifier guidance gradients
comments.quote: The effectiveness of classifier guidance demonstrates that we can obtain powerful generative models from the gradients of a classification function.
comments.explanation: Classifier guidance specifically leverages the gradient of the log-probability output by the classifier, $\nabla_{x_t} \log p_\phi(y|x_t)$, not the gradient of the classification function itself. The classification function typically maps inputs to class labels or logits; taking its gradient directly would not yield the score matching term used in guidance. Tightening this phrasing will accurately reflect the mathematical operation.

comments.title: Compute accessibility claim contradicts method overhead
comments.quote: Our proposed technique makes generative models more accessible in terms of compute costs, especially because new classifiers can be trained and used on top of existing high-quality diffusion models.
comments.explanation: The assertion that classifier guidance improves compute accessibility overlooks two key overheads. Training the required classifier on noisy latents demands significant GPU resources and access to large labeled datasets. Additionally, the guidance mechanism adds a full classifier forward pass at every denoising step, increasing inference latency. Rewriting the claim to specify that the technique reduces the marginal cost of adding conditional control, rather than claiming overall compute accessibility, would align the statement with the actual computational trade-offs.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper presents an architectural refinement of diffusion models and introduces classifier guidance to achieve state-of-the-art image synthesis on ImageNet, effectively closing the performance gap with GANs.

The paper provides a compelling empirical demonstration that diffusion models can outperform GANs in both fidelity and diversity. The systematic ablation of architecture components and the introduction of classifier guidance are significant contributions that have already influenced the trajectory of generative modeling research.

Overview issues:

- Lack of analysis on classifier guidance mode collapse: While Section 4 correctly identifies that classifier guidance allows for a trade-off between fidelity and diversity, it fails to quantify the extent of mode dropping. The paper notes that recall decreases as the guidance scale increases, but it does not investigate whether this loss is uniform across classes or if certain classes are disproportionately dropped. Given that classifier guidance relies on the gradients of a pre-trained classifier, there is a risk that the generative model inherits the classifier biases, leading to a restricted distribution. A more rigorous analysis—perhaps using class-conditional recall metrics or nearest-neighbor analysis in latent space—is needed to ensure the model maintains meaningful coverage of the training distribution.

- Sensitivity to classifier architecture and training: The methodology in Section 4 assumes that the classifier provides stable gradients across all timesteps. However, the paper does not explore how the choice of classifier architecture or the training objective for the classifier affects the stability of the reverse diffusion process. If the classifier is poorly calibrated at high noise levels, the guidance signal could introduce artifacts or instability. Providing an ablation study on the classifier capacity, training noise schedule, or architectural components would strengthen the claim that this method is a general-purpose improvement for diffusion models.

- Computational latency vs. quality trade-off: The paper emphasizes that the proposed models match BigGAN-deep performance with as few as 25 forward passes. However, it acknowledges that each pass is significantly more expensive than a single GAN inference step. The discussion in Section 7 remains qualitative regarding the real-world latency. A more transparent comparison of wall-clock time for a fixed FID target, accounting for both the number of steps and the model parameter count, would be more informative for practitioners. Without this, the claim that diffusion models are now competitive with GANs in practical settings remains slightly ambiguous regarding deployment costs.

- Optimal noise schedule verification: The authors adopt the noise schedule and variance parameterization from previous work without verifying its optimality for the specific high-resolution settings (e.g., 512x512) explored in the paper. As diffusion models scale to higher resolutions, the signal-to-noise ratio at intermediate timesteps becomes increasingly critical. The paper assumes that the schedule tuned for lower resolutions or different datasets remains effective here. Including a sensitivity analysis or a comparison against alternative schedules would provide necessary evidence that the performance gains are not limited by the current choice of noise schedule.

- Quantification of mode coverage and class-conditional distribution shifts: The paper demonstrates that classifier guidance improves FID by trading off recall for precision, but it lacks a granular analysis of which modes are being lost. To substantiate the claim that the model maintains better coverage than GANs, the authors should report class-specific recall or category-wise FID metrics. A specific simulation comparing the class-conditional distribution of the generated samples against the ground truth ImageNet distribution would clarify if the guidance mechanism causes 'class-collapse' for rare or visually complex categories. This is necessary to determine if the reported FID improvements are truly representative of the model's generative capacity across the entire dataset.

- Formal evaluation of the guidance-induced gradient instability: The paper relies on gradients from a classifier $p(y|x_t)$ at various noise levels $t$ to guide the sampling process. However, the stability of these gradients—especially at high noise levels where the signal-to-noise ratio is low—is not empirically verified. The paper should include a diagnostic plot showing the variance of the gradient signal $\nabla_{x_t} \log p(y|x_t)$ across the diffusion trajectory. This would provide necessary evidence that the guidance signal remains well-behaved and does not introduce numerical artifacts or sampling divergence that might be masked by the aggregate FID metric.

Recommendation: accept

Revision targets:

- Include a more detailed analysis of mode coverage, specifically addressing whether specific classes are being dropped as the guidance scale increases.

- Provide a direct wall-clock time comparison against BigGAN-deep to clarify the true computational cost of the 25-step sampling regime.

- Add a brief discussion or supplementary experiment on the sensitivity of the guidance signal to the classifier training noise schedule.

coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
This paper makes a significant empirical contribution by demonstrating that diffusion models, with architectural improvements and classifier guidance, can surpass GANs in image synthesis quality on ImageNet benchmarks. The ablations are thorough, the classifier guidance method is clearly explained, and the results are compelling. However, several methodological concerns—particularly around evaluation fairness, statistical significance, and the completeness of the diversity-fidelity trade-off analysis—need to be addressed to fully substantiate the claims.

Overview issues:

- Lack of statistical significance reporting: The paper reports FID, sFID, IS, Precision, and Recall for each model without any confidence intervals or multiple-run statistics. Given that training diffusion models involves stochasticity (random seeds, data shuffling, weight initialization), a single run's metrics may not be representative. For example, Table 4 shows ADM-G achieving FID 2.97 on ImageNet 128×128, but without error bars, it is unclear whether this is reliably better than BigGAN-deep's 6.02. The authors should report means and standard deviations over at least 3 runs, or justify why a single run is sufficient (e.g., if variance is known to be negligible). This is standard practice in generative model evaluation and is critical for claims of state-of-the-art performance.

- Incomplete diversity-fidelity trade-off characterization: While Figure 3 and Table 3 show the effect of varying the guidance scale on precision and recall, the analysis is limited to a single metric pair and a few scale values. The paper claims that classifier guidance provides a 'knob' to trade off diversity for fidelity, but does not systematically explore the full range of the trade-off or compare it to alternative methods (e.g., truncation trick in GANs) across multiple metrics. Additionally, the recall values in Table 3 drop sharply (e.g., from 0.63 to 0.32 for conditional model with scale 10.0), indicating severe diversity loss, yet the paper does not discuss whether this is acceptable for practical use. The authors should provide precision-recall curves or FID vs. recall plots for a finer grid of guidance scales, and discuss the practical implications of the trade-off.

- Potential unfairness in FID comparisons due to reference statistics: The paper states that it recomputes metrics using the full training set as reference and the same codebase for all models. However, it is not explicitly stated whether the reference statistics (e.g., Inception-V3 activations) are computed identically for the paper's models and the baselines (BigGAN-deep, StyleGAN2). Subtle differences in preprocessing (e.g., resizing method, cropping) can significantly affect FID values. The authors should confirm that the same reference batch and preprocessing pipeline were used for all comparisons, and ideally provide a table showing FID values for baselines under their evaluation setup versus originally reported values. Without this, the claimed improvements may be artifacts of evaluation differences.

- Unclear justification for classifier gradient scaling: The paper introduces a scale factor s on classifier gradients and justifies it as equivalent to raising the classifier distribution to the power s (p(y|x)^s). However, this justification assumes that the classifier is perfectly calibrated and that the gradient direction remains meaningful under scaling. In practice, scaling gradients can lead to distorted samples (e.g., over-saturation, artifacts) as noted in the literature context. The paper does not analyze the sensitivity of sample quality to the choice of s beyond a few values, nor does it discuss how to select s in practice. The authors should provide guidance on choosing s (e.g., based on validation FID) and discuss potential failure modes when s is too large.

- Missing analysis of classifier guidance computational cost: The paper claims classifier guidance is 'compute-efficient' but does not quantify the additional cost of training and evaluating the noisy classifier. The classifier architecture (UNet downsampling trunk) is non-trivial and requires training on the same noising distribution as the diffusion model, which adds significant overhead. Furthermore, during sampling, each step requires a forward pass through the classifier, doubling the compute per step (since the diffusion model also requires a forward pass). The paper should report the total training and sampling compute (e.g., GPU-hours) for the classifier and compare it to the diffusion model alone. Without this, the claim of efficiency is unsubstantiated.

- Limited evaluation of mode coverage beyond recall: The paper uses recall as a proxy for diversity, but recall only measures the fraction of real samples that are covered by generated samples in feature space. It does not capture whether the model generates samples from all modes of the distribution (e.g., rare classes or subcategories). For ImageNet, which has 1000 classes, the paper could compute per-class FID or class-specific precision/recall to demonstrate that the diffusion model does not miss entire classes. The qualitative comparison in Figure 4 is suggestive but not systematic. The authors should include a quantitative analysis of mode coverage, such as the number of classes with non-zero generated samples or per-class FID distributions.

- No demonstration that classifier guidance is unbiased: The paper claims classifier guidance provides a principled way to condition diffusion models, but never verifies that the guided sampling process produces samples that are consistent with the true conditional distribution p(x|y). The derivation in Appendix D.2 shows that adding classifier gradients to the unconditional score yields a conditional score only if the classifier is perfectly calibrated and the scale s=1. When s>1, the paper acknowledges this corresponds to sampling from p(x) * p(y|x)^s, which is not the true conditional distribution. The authors should provide a quantitative analysis showing how the KL divergence between the guided distribution and the true conditional varies with s, or at minimum show that for s=1 the samples match the class-conditional distribution on a simple dataset where the true conditional is known.

- Missing comparison to alternative conditioning methods: The paper positions classifier guidance as a method for conditional image synthesis, but never compares it to the standard approach of directly conditioning the diffusion model on class labels via adaptive group normalization (which the paper already uses). Table 3 shows that adding guidance to an already conditional model improves FID from 10.94 to 4.59, but it is unclear whether this improvement is due to the guidance itself or simply because the classifier provides a stronger class signal. The authors should include an ablation where they train a conditional diffusion model with increased class embedding dimension or additional class-conditional layers to match the classifier's capacity, and compare the resulting FID. Without this, the paper cannot claim that classifier guidance is the key factor driving the improvement.

- No analysis of classifier guidance failure modes: The paper introduces a scale factor s on classifier gradients and shows it trades off diversity for fidelity, but never discusses what happens when s is chosen poorly. For large s, the paper's own results show recall dropping to 0.32 (Table 3), indicating severe mode collapse. The authors should provide examples of failure cases (e.g., samples with artifacts, over-saturation, or incorrect class assignment) for a range of s values, and discuss how practitioners can detect and avoid these failure modes. Additionally, the paper should analyze whether the classifier remains well-calibrated under the noising distribution used during sampling, as miscalibration could lead to biased gradients.

- Incomplete discussion of computational cost for classifier guidance: The paper claims classifier guidance is 'compute-efficient' but never quantifies the additional cost. Training a noisy classifier on ImageNet requires a separate training run with its own hyperparameters and compute budget. During sampling, each step requires a forward pass through both the diffusion model and the classifier, doubling the per-step compute. The authors should report the total GPU-hours for training the classifier and the per-sample sampling time with and without guidance. Without this, the claim of efficiency is unsubstantiated and readers cannot assess the practical trade-off between improved FID and increased computational cost.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Report FID, sFID, IS, Precision, and Recall with confidence intervals (e.g., over 3 runs) for all main results to establish statistical significance.

- Provide a systematic analysis of the diversity-fidelity trade-off with a finer grid of guidance scales and precision-recall curves, and discuss practical implications.

- Clarify the evaluation protocol: confirm that reference statistics and preprocessing are identical for all models, and report FID for baselines under the paper's evaluation setup.

- Quantify the computational cost of classifier guidance (training and sampling) and discuss its efficiency relative to the diffusion model alone.

- Include a quantitative mode coverage analysis (e.g., per-class FID or number of classes covered) to support the claim of better distribution coverage.

comments.title: Algorithm 2 missing gradient scale s
comments.quote:
Algorithm 2 Classifier guided DDIM sampling, given a diffusion model  $\epsilon_{\theta}(x_t)$ , classifier  $p_{\phi}(y|x_t)$  and gradient scale  $s$ .
Input: class label  $y$  gradient scale s
$x_{T}\gets$  sample from  $\mathcal{N}(0,\mathbf{I})$
for all  $t$  from  $T$  to 1 do
$\hat{\epsilon} \leftarrow \epsilon_{\theta}(x_t) - \sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_t} \nabla_{x_t} \log p_{\phi}(y|x_t)$
$x_{t-1} \leftarrow \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_{t-1}} \left( \frac{x_t - \sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_t} \hat{\epsilon}}{\sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}} \right) + \sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_{t-1}} \hat{\epsilon}$
end for
return  $x_0$
comments.explanation: Algorithm 2 takes a gradient scale s as input but never uses it. In Algorithm 1, the scale s is explicitly applied: μ + sΣ ∇_x_t log p_φ(y|x_t). For consistency, Algorithm 2 should include s in the definition of ε̂. The correct update should be: ε̂ ← ε_θ(x_t) - s √(1 - ᾱ_t) ∇_x_t log p_φ(y|x_t). Without this, the scale parameter has no effect on DDIM sampling, contradicting the paper's discussion of tuning s. Add s to the ε̂ definition in Algorithm 2.

comments.title: Identical sFID values for ADM and ADM-G on ImageNet 128x128
comments.explanation: The table reports sFID = 5.09 for both ADM and ADM-G on ImageNet 128x128. This is suspicious because classifier guidance changes the sample distribution, so identical sFID is unlikely. The paper should either explain this coincidence (e.g., rounding to two decimals hides a difference, or a specific property of the metric) or correct the value. Rewrite the ADM-G sFID entry to a value that differs from ADM's unless a valid reason is provided, and add a note explaining the invariance.

comments.title: Guidance scale not specified for ADM-G entries
comments.explanation: The table does not state the guidance scale used for ADM-G. Section 4 shows that for a specific class (Pembroke Welsh corgi), guidance scale 1.0 gives FID 33.0 and scale 10.0 gives FID 12.0, which are worse than the unconditional model. The dramatic improvement to FID 2.97 on the full ImageNet 128x128 suggests a different scale or class-conditional setting. The paper should specify the guidance scale for each ADM-G entry and explain why class-specific examples show worse FID while overall FID improves. Add a footnote or column indicating the guidance scale used.

comments.title: ADM-G recall on ImageNet 512x512 contradicts diversity claim
comments.explanation: The table shows ADM-G recall 0.42 on ImageNet 512x512, lower than ADM's 0.60. The paper claims diffusion models 'maintain a higher coverage of the distribution as measured by recall' compared to GANs. While ADM-G's recall (0.42) is higher than BigGAN-deep's 0.29, it is substantially lower than ADM's 0.60. The paper should clarify that the claim of higher recall applies to the unconditional model (ADM) but not necessarily to the guided model (ADM-G), or discuss the trade-off explicitly. The current text is misleading because it implies all diffusion models have higher recall, but ADM-G sacrifices recall for precision.

comments.title: ADM-G recall on ImageNet 256x256 also lower than ADM
comments.explanation: On ImageNet 256x256, ADM-G recall is 0.52 (or 0.49 with 25 steps), compared to ADM's 0.63. This is consistent with the trade-off noted in comment 3, but the paper's claim about higher recall is not qualified. The text should explicitly state that the recall advantage applies to the unconditional model, while guided models trade recall for precision. Add a sentence in Section 5 clarifying this trade-off.

comments.title: Table 1 row formatting obscures values
comments.explanation: The compact table format is ambiguous: it is unclear whether each row represents a single change from the baseline or cumulative changes. For example, the row with Heads=4 shows FID differences -0.54 and -0.82, but it is not specified whether Depth remains 2 (as in baseline) or 4 (as in the previous row). This ambiguity undermines the claim of a 'positive compounding effect' because the reader cannot verify which combinations were tested. The paper should restructure the table so that each row lists all parameter values explicitly, or use a clear notation (e.g., bold or underline) to indicate which parameters differ from the baseline. Rewrite the table as a full grid with all columns filled for every row, or add a footnote explaining that blank cells inherit the value from the first row of the block.

comments.title: Table 2 missing baseline FID and channel counts
comments.explanation: The table header states 'Channels per head' but the first four rows leave this column blank, implying a default value that is never defined. The text says the base model is 'a smaller version of the best model from Table 1', but does not specify the number of channels per head for that base model. Without this information, the FID differences (e.g., -0.50, -0.97) are uninterpretable because the reader cannot determine the baseline FID or the absolute channel count. The paper should explicitly state the base model's channels per head (likely 128, given the pattern) and fill in the missing values in the table. Additionally, the row with 'Channels per head = 32' shows FID -1.36, but it is unclear whether this corresponds to 1 head or some other number of heads. The table should either use separate rows for each combination or clearly indicate the number of heads when varying channels per head.

comments.title: AdaGN definition missing normalization axis
comments.quote: We define this layer as  $\mathrm{AdaGN}(h,y) = y_s$  GroupNorm  $(h) + y_b$ , where  $h$  is the intermediate activations of the residual block following the first convolution, and  $y = [y_s,y_b]$  is obtained from a linear projection of the timestep and class embedding.
comments.explanation: The definition of AdaGN is ambiguous: it writes $y_s$ GroupNorm $(h) + y_b$, but does not specify whether $y_s$ and $y_b$ are scalars, vectors, or tensors, nor how they are broadcast over the spatial and channel dimensions of $h$. Without this specification, the operation is mathematically underspecified. For example, if $h$ has shape [batch, channels, height, width] and $y_s$ is a scalar, the multiplication is well-defined; but if $y_s$ is a vector of length equal to the number of channels, the broadcasting rule must be stated. The paper should clarify the shapes of $y_s$ and $y_b$ and the broadcasting semantics, e.g., 'where $y_s$ and $y_b$ are vectors of length equal to the number of channels in $h$, and are broadcast over spatial dimensions.'

comments.title: Unsupported claim about guidance and upsampling combination
comments.quote: When combining guidance with upsampling, we only guide the lower resolution model.
comments.explanation: The paper reports that combining classifier guidance with upsampling improves FID (e.g., from 4.59 to 3.94 on ImageNet 256×256), but provides no ablation or theoretical rationale for guiding only the lower-resolution model. Without comparing to alternatives (e.g., guiding both models or only the higher-resolution model), the reader cannot assess whether this design choice is optimal or robust. The authors should add an ablation study comparing guidance on the lower model vs. both models vs. the higher model, or cite a theoretical reason for this design.

comments.title: Incomplete comparison of diversity-fidelity trade-off methods
comments.quote: One missing element from previous work on diffusion models is a way to trade off diversity for fidelity. Other generative techniques provide natural levers for this trade-off. Brock et al. [8] introduced the truncation trick for GANs, wherein the latent vector is sampled from a truncated normal distribution. They found that increasing truncation naturally led to a decrease in diversity but an increase in fidelity. More recently, Razavi et al. [57] proposed to use classifier rejection sampling to filter out bad samples from an autoregressive likelihood-based model, and found that this technique improved FID. DeVries et al. [16] found that filtering out low-density regions of the training set improves GAN training performance. Most likelihood-based models also allow for low-temperature sampling [1], which provides a natural way to emphasize modes of the data distribution (see Appendix I).
comments.explanation: The paper claims classifier guidance provides a 'knob' for the diversity-fidelity trade-off, but does not quantitatively compare this trade-off to the alternatives it lists (truncation trick, rejection sampling, low-temperature sampling). For example, precision-recall curves or FID vs. recall plots for classifier guidance vs. truncation trick on the same architecture would substantiate the claim. The authors should include a direct comparison of the diversity-fidelity trade-off achieved by classifier guidance versus at least one listed alternative on a common benchmark.

comments.title: Missing discussion of classifier guidance computational cost
comments.quote: , 2]*. Our proposed classifier guidance technique is currently limited to labeled datasets. In the future, our method could be extended to unlabeled data by clustering samples to produce synthetic labels *[42]* or by training discriminative models to use for guidance.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims classifier guidance is 'compute-efficient,' but the paper never quantifies the additional computational cost of training and evaluating the noisy classifier. Training a classifier on noisy images at all noise levels requires a separate training run with its own hyperparameters and compute budget. During sampling, each step requires a forward pass through both the diffusion model and the classifier, doubling the per-step compute. The paper should report total GPU-hours for training the classifier and per-sample sampling time with and without guidance to substantiate the efficiency claim.

comments.title: Missing definition of L_simple and L_vlb
comments.quote: Ho et al. *[31]* observe that the simple mean-squared error objective, $L_{\text{simple}}$, works better in practice than the actual variational lower bound $L_{\text{vlb}}$ that can be derived from interpreting the denoising diffusion model as a VAE.
comments.explanation: The text introduces $L_{\text{simple}}$ and $L_{\text{vlb}}$ without defining them. While $L_{\text{simple}}$ is implicitly the mean-squared error loss described earlier, $L_{\text{vlb}}$ is not defined at all. This omission makes the section self-contained only for readers already familiar with the cited works. The authors should add a brief definition: e.g., '$L_{\text{simple}} = \mathbb{E}_{t,x_0,\epsilon}[\|\epsilon_\theta(x_t,t)-\epsilon\|^2]$ and $L_{\text{vlb}}$ is the variational lower bound on the negative log-likelihood (see Appendix C for details).'

comments.title: Normalization constant clarification needed
comments.quote: To understand the effect of scaling classifier gradients, note that  $s \cdot \nabla_x \log p(y|x) = \nabla_x \log \frac{1}{Z} p(y|x)^s$ , where  $Z$  is an arbitrary constant. As a result, the conditioning process is still theoretically grounded in a re-normalized classifier distribution proportional to  $p(y|x)^s$ .
comments.explanation: The statement 'Z is an arbitrary constant' is misleading. If Z is the normalizing constant of p(y|x)^s, it depends on x and its gradient is not zero. However, the authors' intended meaning is that adding a constant to the log-density does not affect the gradient, so the derivation is mathematically valid if Z is treated as a constant with respect to x. The passage would benefit from clarifying that Z is a constant with respect to the variable of differentiation, not an arbitrary constant. Rewrite the quoted text as: 'note that s * ∇_x log p(y|x) = ∇_x log p(y|x)^s, and since adding a constant to the log-density does not affect the gradient, the conditioning process corresponds to sampling from a distribution proportional to p(y|x)^s.'

comments.title: Unsupported claim about metric-human judgment correspondence
comments.quote: While these metrics are often used in practice and correspond well with human judgement, they are not a perfect proxy, and finding better metrics for sample quality evaluation is still an open problem.
comments.explanation: The claim that FID, sFID, Precision, Recall, and IS 'correspond well with human judgement' is not supported by any citation in this section. While FID is known to correlate reasonably with human perception in some settings (e.g., [29]), the correspondence is not universally established, especially for sFID and the specific precision/recall variants used. The authors should either provide a citation supporting this claim (e.g., [29] for FID, [38] for precision/recall) or soften the language to 'are widely used and generally correlate with human perception in many settings' to avoid overstatement.

comments.title: Algorithm 1 missing gradient scale in notation
comments.quote:
Algorithm 1 Classifier guided diffusion sampling, given a diffusion model  $(\mu_{\theta}(x_t),\Sigma_{\theta}(x_t))$  , classifier  $p_{\phi}(y|x_t)$  , and gradient scale s.
Input: class label  $y$  gradient scale s
$x_{T}\gets$  sample from  $\mathcal{N}(0,\mathbf{I})$
for all  $t$  from  $T$  to 1 do
$\mu, \Sigma \leftarrow \mu_{\theta}(x_t), \Sigma_{\theta}(x_t)$
$x_{t-1} \leftarrow \text{sample from } \mathcal{N}(\mu + s\Sigma \nabla_{x_t} \log p_{\phi}(y|x_t), \Sigma)$
end for
return  $x_0$
comments.explanation: Algorithm 1 uses the notation Σ for the covariance matrix, but the update uses sΣ ∇_x_t log p_φ(y|x_t). This implicitly assumes the covariance is diagonal or that the gradient is multiplied elementwise. For a general covariance matrix, the correct mean shift should be s Σ ∇_x_t log p_φ(y|x_t), which is what is written. However, the paper does not specify whether Σ is diagonal or full. In practice, diffusion models often use diagonal covariances, but this should be stated explicitly. Add a note that Σ is diagonal or that the multiplication is elementwise.

coarse__glm-4.7-flash.label: Coarse (glm-4.7-flash)
coarse__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback:
This paper presents significant empirical improvements to diffusion models, achieving state-of-the-art FID scores on ImageNet. However, the manuscript lacks the theoretical and architectural analysis required to justify these results. The 'Architecture Improvements' section is purely empirical, and the methodological contributions, particularly classifier guidance and its interaction with upsampling, are presented as facts without explaining the underlying mechanisms.

The paper makes a compelling empirical contribution by closing the gap between diffusion models and GANs on high-resolution image synthesis. The FID scores reported are impressive and the ablation studies provide a clear picture of what architectural changes work. However, the paper fails to explain *why* these changes work or how the proposed methods behave in edge cases, making it difficult to assess the robustness of the claims.

Overview issues:

- Architecture section lacks mechanistic explanation: Section 3 presents a laundry list of architectural modifications—BigGAN residual blocks, attention at multiple resolutions, and AdaGN—without explaining the architectural rationale or the theoretical motivation for these choices. The section relies entirely on ablation tables (Table 1 and Table 2) to demonstrate that these changes improve FID, but it never explains *why* a BigGAN residual block helps diffusion models or why 64 channels per head is optimal. This makes the contribution feel like a collection of hacks rather than a coherent architectural insight. The paper would benefit from a discussion of how these architectural choices affect the model's ability to capture long-range dependencies or process high-frequency details, which are known challenges in diffusion models.

comments.title: Justification for 64 channels per head
comments.quote: On wall-clock (Figure 5, Appendix A.1), we see that 64 channels is best so we opt to use 64 channels per head as our default.
comments.explanation: The authors select 64 channels per head based on wall-clock efficiency and alignment with Transformer architectures. However, this choice is presented as an empirical optimum without a deeper discussion of the trade-offs involved. A more rigorous justification is needed to explain why this specific configuration yields the best FID, rather than treating it as a hyperparameter selection.
